<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines variables and functions. The global/top-level scope contains only variable assignments (pkgname, pkgver, source, checksums, etc.) and no command substitutions or dangerous operations. The `pkgver` function is not defined, so no code executes during sourcing beyond setting variables. The `source` array uses HTTPS URLs from the official TablePlus repository (`deb.tableplus.com`) with pinned checksums, which is standard practice. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are present but will not be executed under the `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` flags used for this gate. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests to unexpected hosts, and no remote code execution. Fetching the declared sources is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code in scope; fetching sources is safe.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/tableplus.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-ssnvx0i0...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources that fetch pre-built Debian packages from the official TablePlus domain (deb.tableplus.com) for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The `source` arrays use structured URLs pointing to specific versions, and the checksums are provided, ensuring integrity verification during the download phase. The URLs are over HTTPS and from the project's own domain, which is expected for an official package.

The top-level scope of the PKGBUILD contains no command substitutions, function calls, or inline scripts outside of static assignments. The `pkgver` is hardcoded and does not involve version fetching from a remote source. As a result, sourcing the PKGBUILD during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will not execute any untrusted code or trigger network requests beyond downloading the declared sources.

While the use of `.deb` archives containing pre-compiled binaries limits source-level auditing, this does not constitute a supply-chain attack in this context. The commands under evaluation only fetch and verify sources — they do not execute code from the `prepare()` or `package()` functions. Thus, running the specified makepkg commands is safe with respect to immediate code execution or data exfiltration risks.
</details>
<summary>Safe: no remote code execution during source fetch</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: no remote code execution during source fetch[/]

LLM auditresponse for tableplus.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `tableplus.desktop` is a standard desktop entry file used to integrate applications into desktop environments. It declares metadata such as the application name, icon path, execution command, and categories. The `Exec` field points to `/usr/local/bin/tableplus`, a typical binary installation path, and the `Icon` field references a local resource under `/opt/tableplus`, which is a conventional location for third-party software. There are no network operations, script executions, or use of dangerous constructs like `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, or `base64`. All fields contain static, expected values consistent with legitimate application integration.

This file does not execute any code on its own and poses no direct security risk. It is used solely by the desktop environment to display and launch the application. The paths specified are absolute and point to locations typically used for manually installed software, which is common for proprietary or AUR-installed applications. No evidence of obfuscation, data exfiltration, or malicious redirection is present.

While `/usr/local/bin` and `/opt` are less common in standard packaging (where `/usr/bin` and `/usr/share` are typical), this deviation does not indicate malice. It may reflect a vendor-specific installation method. Such layout choices are outside the scope of supply-chain security unless combined with suspicious behavior, which is absent here.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry with no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed tableplus.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry with no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches pre-built Debian packages from the official TablePlus domain (`deb.tableplus.com`) for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, which is consistent with the package's upstream distribution model. The sources are properly checksummed using provided `sha256sums_x86_64` and `sha256sums_aarch64` values, ensuring integrity. The `prepare()` function extracts a `.tar.zst` file from the deb's data, which is standard for repackaging. The `package()` function installs binaries, desktop files, and licenses into appropriate directories under `$pkgdir`, following standard Arch Linux packaging practices.

There are no signs of obfuscated code, unexpected network requests, or dangerous commands. The script does not execute arbitrary remote code, exfiltrate data, or modify system files outside the package environment. The use of `echo -e` to create a wrapper script is transparent and minimal. All actions serve the purpose of repackaging the official binary for Arch Linux. While the use of pre-built binaries from upstream (instead of compiling from source) is a hygiene consideration, it is not inherently malicious, especially when checksums are provided and verified.

This PKGBUILD adheres to typical AUR packaging patterns for binary applications and shows no evidence of supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Official binaries with valid checksums; no malicious behavior detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for _gpgbuilder:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `_gpgbuilder` contains a PGP-signed message that verifies the integrity and authenticity of package component files (e.g., `debian-binary`, `control.tar.zst`, `data.tar.zst`) by listing their checksums, sizes, and hashes. The signature is valid for the listed files and is signed by a known builder key (Signer: 2A17818B38772786). This is a standard practice in secure package building to prevent tampering during distribution. There is no executable code, network activity, or system modification present in the file. The content serves only as a verification manifest and poses no security threat.
</details>
<summary>
PGP-signed manifest; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for LICENSE:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is the END USER LICENSE AGREEMENT (EULA) for TablePlus, a database management tool. It outlines the legal terms and conditions under which the software may be used, including licensing restrictions, ownership, privacy policy, warranty disclaimers, and liability limitations. The document contains no executable code, scripts, or commands. It is a standard legal text distributed with software to inform users of their rights and obligations.

There are no signs of malicious content, obfuscation, or harmful instructions in this file. The presence of a URL (https://tableplus.com/blog/2018/01/license-agreement.html) is appropriate for referencing the online version of the license and does not constitute a security risk. References to privacy policy and third-party services are typical in modern software licenses.

This file serves purely informational and legal purposes and poses no threat to system security. It is not involved in the installation, build, or runtime processes of the software in a way that could introduce supply chain attacks. Therefore, it is safe.
</details>
<summary>
Legal document with no executable code; SAFE
</summary>
</security_assessment>
